summary:
Porcelain typically lacks large concentrations of substances like calcium carbonate due to its specific manufacturing process aimed at achieving unique mechanical characteristics compared to other types of ceramics which may contain such components as part of their formulation for different purposes.
The presence of certain minerals can significantly impact a material’s strength, translucency, thermal expansion coefficient etc., making precise control over ingredients crucial during fabrication processes tailored uniquely toward producing high-quality restorations using porcelains designed primarily based upon glass-ceramic technology principles without relying heavily on calcite content found more commonly elsewhere within broader categories encompassing white wares including some forms traditionally referred too simply under 'ceramics'. To summarize concisely while focusing strictly clinically-relevant aspects per your instruction:

Porcelain generally excludes major proportions of elements like calcium carbonate because it uses specialized techniques emphasizing particular attributes unlike conventional pottery where those constituents might be utilized differently affecting overall qualities produced through alternative methodologies targeting diverse applications beyond just aesthetic restoration needs alone involving biocompatibility considerations paramount when selecting appropriate biomaterials suitable across wide range medical devices requiring stringent performance standards met consistently throughout
